expanded_description:
Central banks have a toolkit of instruments to influence the money supply, interest rates, and ultimately inflation and employment. The three traditional tools—open market operations, the discount rate, and reserve requirements—have been used for decades, though their relative importance has shifted over time. Open market operations are the most frequently used tool in normal times; the discount rate serves as a backstop liquidity facility; reserve requirements have been reduced or eliminated in many countries.

Understanding monetary policy tools is essential for analyzing how central banks implement policy decisions, how changes in policy rates transmit to financial markets, and why some tools are more effective than others in different economic environments. The Federal Reserve and other major central banks have adapted their toolkits over time: before 2008, they relied primarily on open market operations to target the federal funds rate; after 2008, with abundant reserves, they shifted to a floor system using interest on reserves (IOR) as the primary tool, with the discount window and reverse repo facility as supporting tools.

In this section, we will examine each tool in detail: open market operations (repos, reverse repos, outright purchases/sales), the discount window (primary credit, secondary credit, seasonal credit), and reserve requirements (required reserves, excess reserves, the money multiplier). We will analyze how these tools work, their effectiveness in different regimes (scarce vs. ample reserves), and how central banks have adapted their toolkits since the 2008 financial crisis. You will learn to calculate the effects of open market operations on reserves, understand the role of the discount window as a lender of last resort, and evaluate why reserve requirements have fallen out of favor.

core_concept.principle:
Central banks have three traditional monetary policy tools to influence the money supply and interest rates:

1. Open Market Operations (OMOs): The purchase or sale of government securities by the central bank in the open market. Expansionary OMO (purchase of bonds) injects reserves into the banking system, increasing the money supply and lowering interest rates. Contractionary OMO (sale of bonds) drains reserves, decreasing the money supply and raising interest rates.

2. Discount Rate (Discount Window): The interest rate charged by the central bank when it lends reserves to commercial banks. Banks borrow from the discount window when they face temporary liquidity shortages. A lower discount rate encourages borrowing, increasing reserves and the money supply. A higher discount rate discourages borrowing, decreasing reserves. The discount rate is typically set above the policy rate (penalty rate) to encourage banks to use private markets first.

3. Reserve Requirements: Regulations that require banks to hold a minimum fraction of deposits as reserves (either vault cash or deposits at the central bank). Lowering reserve requirements reduces required reserves, freeing up excess reserves for lending, increasing the money multiplier and money supply. Raising reserve requirements increases required reserves, reducing excess reserves and the money multiplier.

Modern Tools (Post-2008):
• Interest on Reserve Balances (IORB): Interest paid by the Fed on bank reserves. Acts as a floor under the federal funds rate.
• Overnight Reverse Repo Facility (ON RRP): Allows non-banks (money market funds) to lend reserves to the Fed, setting a floor for non-banks.
• Standing Repo Facility (SRF): A backstop for repo markets, providing liquidity when needed.
• Quantitative Easing (QE): Large-scale purchases of long-term securities to lower long-term interest rates when short rates are at zero.

Policy Implementation Frameworks:
• Scarce reserves (pre-2008): Active OMOs to fine-tune reserve supply; discount window as ceiling; no IOR.
• Ample reserves/floor system (post-2008): IORB as floor; OMOs minimal; discount window as backstop; ON RRP as additional floor for non-banks.
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine the central bank wants to lower interest rates to stimulate the economy. It conducts an open market purchase: it buys government bonds from primary dealers, paying with newly created reserves. The dealers' bank accounts increase, so bank reserves rise. With more reserves, banks have excess lending capacity. They lend more, creating new deposits, which increases the money supply. The increased supply of reserves pushes the federal funds rate down (the price of reserves falls). Lower interest rates encourage borrowing and spending, boosting aggregate demand.

If the central bank wants to raise interest rates to fight inflation, it does the opposite: it sells bonds, draining reserves, reducing lending, decreasing the money supply, and pushing interest rates up.

The discount window works as a safety valve. If a bank unexpectedly runs low on reserves (due to a large withdrawal), it can borrow from the Fed's discount window at the discount rate. This prevents the bank from failing or causing a spike in the federal funds rate. The discount rate is usually set above the federal funds rate target to encourage banks to borrow from other banks first (the discount window is a 'lender of last resort,' not a routine funding source).

Reserve requirements were once a key tool. By raising the reserve requirement, the Fed forces banks to hold more reserves, reducing their ability to lend. This lowers the money multiplier and contracts the money supply. However, reserve requirements are now rarely changed; many central banks (including the Fed in 2020) have set them to zero, relying instead on interest on reserves (IOR) to control the money supply.

image.alt: Diagram: Three traditional tools (OMOs, discount rate, reserve requirements) with arrows showing their effects on reserves, money supply, and interest rates; modern tools (IORB, ON RRP, QE) shown in separate box.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.1: The central bank's monetary policy toolkit: traditional tools (OMOs, discount rate, reserve requirements) and modern tools (IORB, ON RRP, QE).

sections.introduction:
Central banks have a toolkit of instruments to influence the money supply, interest rates, and ultimately inflation and employment. The three traditional tools—open market operations, the discount rate, and reserve requirements—have been used for decades, though their relative importance has shifted over time. Open market operations are the most frequently used tool in normal times; the discount rate serves as a backstop liquidity facility; reserve requirements have been reduced or eliminated in many countries.

Understanding monetary policy tools is essential for analyzing how central banks implement policy decisions, how changes in policy rates transmit to financial markets, and why some tools are more effective than others in different economic environments. The Federal Reserve and other major central banks have adapted their toolkits over time: before 2008, they relied primarily on open market operations to target the federal funds rate; after 2008, with abundant reserves, they shifted to a floor system using interest on reserves (IOR) as the primary tool, with the discount window and reverse repo facility as supporting tools.

In this section, we will examine each tool in detail: open market operations (repos, reverse repos, outright purchases/sales), the discount window (primary credit, secondary credit, seasonal credit), and reserve requirements (required reserves, excess reserves, the money multiplier). We will analyze how these tools work, their effectiveness in different regimes (scarce vs. ample reserves), and how central banks have adapted their toolkits since the 2008 financial crisis.

introduction_image.alt: Timeline: Pre-2008 (OMOs primary, discount rate backstop, reserve requirements active); 2008-2014 (ZLB, QE introduced, IOR introduced); 2015-2019 (normalization, ON RRP); 2020-present (zero reserve requirements, SRF).
introduction_image.caption: Figure 8.1.2: Evolution of monetary policy tools: the 2008 crisis led to the introduction of IOR, QE, and the ON RRP facility.

key_concept.definition:
Monetary policy tools are the instruments central banks use to implement monetary policy, influencing the money supply, interest rates, and credit conditions.

1. Open Market Operations (OMOs):
• Expansionary (purchase): Central bank buys government securities, paying with reserves → reserves increase → money supply increases → interest rates fall.
• Contractionary (sale): Central bank sells government securities, receiving reserves → reserves decrease → money supply decreases → interest rates rise.
• Temporary OMOs (repos/reverse repos): Short-term (overnight to a few weeks) to manage daily liquidity.
• Permanent OMOs (outright purchases/sales): Long-term changes to the central bank's securities portfolio.

2. Discount Rate (Discount Window):
• Primary credit: Overnight loans to healthy banks at a rate above the policy rate (penalty rate).
• Secondary credit: Loans to banks not eligible for primary credit (financial distress), at a higher rate.
• Seasonal credit: Loans to small banks with seasonal liquidity needs (e.g., agricultural banks).
• The discount rate acts as a ceiling for overnight interest rates (banks will not pay more than the discount rate).

3. Reserve Requirements:
• Required reserves: $RR = rr \times D$, where $rr$ is the reserve ratio and $D$ is deposits.
• Excess reserves: Actual reserves minus required reserves.
• Money multiplier: $m = (1+c)/(rr+c+e)$. Lowering $rr$ increases $m$, expanding money supply.
• Many central banks (Canada, UK, Australia, Sweden, Fed in 2020) have eliminated reserve requirements.

Modern Tools (Post-2008):
• Interest on Reserve Balances (IORB): The Fed pays interest on reserves. Acts as a floor for the federal funds rate.
• Overnight Reverse Repo Facility (ON RRP): Allows non-banks to lend reserves to the Fed, setting a floor for non-banks.
• Standing Repo Facility (SRF): A backstop for repo markets, providing liquidity when needed.
• Quantitative Easing (QE): Large-scale asset purchases to lower long-term interest rates when short rates are at the ZLB.
key_concept.context: The Fed's toolkit has evolved dramatically. From 1913 to 2008, open market operations were the primary tool, with the discount window as a backstop and reserve requirements occasionally used. The 2008 crisis changed everything: the Fed cut rates to zero (ZLB), introduced IOR (2008), created the ON RRP facility (2013), engaged in multiple rounds of QE (2008-2014, 2020-2022), and created the SRF (2021). Reserve requirements were set to zero in 2020. Today, the Fed operates an 'ample reserves' framework where IORB is the primary tool, OMOs are used only for occasional fine-tuning, and the discount window serves as a rarely-used backstop due to stigma. The ECB and other central banks have similar toolkits but with different emphases (e.g., the ECB uses negative deposit facility rates).

image.alt: Diagram: Central bank tools → reserves/money supply/interest rates → inflation/output. Traditional tools box: OMOs, discount rate, reserve requirements. Modern tools box: IORB, ON RRP, QE, SRF.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.3: Monetary policy tools affect the money supply, interest rates, and credit conditions, which ultimately influence inflation and output.

classifications.value: The most frequently used tool; purchases and sales of government securities to adjust reserves and interest rates
characteristics: Expansionary: Central bank buys bonds → reserves increase → money supply increases → interest rates fall
Contractionary: Central bank sells bonds → reserves decrease → money supply decreases → interest rates rise
Temporary (repos/reverse repos): Short-term liquidity management (overnight to several weeks)
Permanent (outright purchases/sales): Long-term changes to central bank balance sheet
Primary dealers: The only counterparties for Fed OMOs (large banks and securities firms)
Pre-2008: Daily fine-tuning operations; Post-2008: Minimal use due to ample reserves framework
classifications.behavior:
Open market operations are the central bank's most flexible and precise tool. In a scarce reserves framework (pre-2008), the Fed conducted daily OMOs to keep the federal funds rate at its target. If reserves were too low, the Fed bought bonds (added reserves); if reserves were too high, the Fed sold bonds (drained reserves). The trading desk at the New York Fed would announce a repo or reverse repo operation each morning, primary dealers would submit bids, and the desk would accept bids until the desired reserve level was achieved.

In an ample reserves framework (post-2008), the Fed no longer fine-tunes reserves daily. Instead, it sets IORB (interest on reserves) and lets the market determine reserve demand. OMOs are used only for large adjustments (e.g., during the 2019 repo spike) or as part of QE (large-scale asset purchases). The Standing Repo Facility (SRF) now provides a backstop, so discretionary OMOs are rare.

Quantitative easing (QE) is an extreme form of OMOs: the central bank buys long-term bonds (not just short-term Treasuries) in large quantities to lower long-term interest rates. QE was used extensively after the 2008 crisis and again during the 2020 pandemic. It expands the central bank's balance sheet dramatically and is intended to stimulate the economy when short-term rates are at zero.

image.alt: Graph: Expansionary OMO (bond purchase) shifts money supply right, lowering interest rate from i1 to i2. Contractionary OMO (bond sale) shifts money supply left, raising interest rate.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.4: Open market operations shift the money supply curve, changing the equilibrium interest rate.

examples.explanation: Fed buys \$10 billion in Treasury bonds from a primary dealer. The Fed pays by crediting the dealer's bank account with \$10 billion in reserves. Bank reserves increase by \$10 billion. With more reserves, banks lend more, the money supply expands, and the federal funds rate falls from 5.25% to 5.00%.

Fed sells \$10 billion in Treasury bonds to a primary dealer. The dealer pays by debiting its bank account, reducing reserves by \$10 billion. Bank reserves decrease, lending contracts, the money supply falls, and the federal funds rate rises from 5.00% to 5.25%.

In March 2020, the Fed announced unlimited QE, buying \$120 billion per month of Treasury bonds and mortgage-backed securities. This expanded the Fed's balance sheet from \$4 trillion to \$7 trillion. Long-term interest rates fell (10-year Treasury yield from 1.5% to 0.5%), stimulating housing and investment when short rates were already at zero.

classifications.value: The interest rate at which commercial banks borrow reserves directly from the central bank; a backstop liquidity facility
characteristics: Primary credit: Overnight loans to healthy banks at a penalty rate (usually 50bp above the policy rate)
Secondary credit: Loans to banks not eligible for primary credit (financial distress), at a higher rate (usually 100bp above)
Seasonal credit: Loans to small banks with seasonal liquidity needs (agricultural, tourist areas)
Acts as a ceiling for overnight interest rates (banks won't pay more than the discount rate)
Stigma: Banks avoid using the discount window due to fear of signaling weakness
Lender of last resort function: Prevents bank runs and liquidity crises (Bagehot's rule)
classifications.behavior:
The discount window is the Fed's lender of last resort facility. It provides liquidity to banks that face temporary funding shortfalls—for example, a sudden deposit outflow or an unexpected large payment. Banks can borrow reserves overnight from the Fed by pledging collateral (Treasuries, agency MBS, loans). The discount rate is typically set above the federal funds rate target to discourage routine use; banks should use private markets first and turn to the discount window only in emergencies.

Despite its importance, the discount window is rarely used due to stigma. Banks fear that if they borrow from the discount window, markets will perceive them as weak, triggering a run or a stock price decline. This stigma was acute during the 2008 crisis: healthy banks refused to borrow from the discount window even when they needed liquidity, forcing the Fed to create emergency facilities (PDCF, TSLF) with different names to avoid stigma.

The Fed has tried to reduce stigma by renaming the facility (from 'adjustment credit' to 'primary credit'), simplifying documentation, and publicly encouraging banks to borrow. However, stigma persists. During the 2023 regional banking crisis (Silicon Valley Bank, Signature Bank), banks again avoided the discount window, borrowing instead from the Fed's new Bank Term Funding Program (BTFP). The discount window remains a critical backstop but is underutilized due to stigma.

image.alt: Graph: Federal funds rate (market rate) below discount rate (ceiling). Spread shows penalty rate. When market rate approaches discount rate, banks borrow from discount window, capping rates.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.5: The discount rate acts as a ceiling for overnight interest rates; banks will not pay more than the discount rate.

examples.process: Bank Uses Discount Window During Liquidity Shortage
examples.explanation: A bank unexpectedly loses \$50 million in reserves due to a large wire transfer out. It cannot borrow from other banks because all are tight. To avoid defaulting on its payment obligations, it borrows \$50 million from the Fed's discount window at the primary credit rate (fed funds target + 50bp). The loan is repaid the next day. The bank avoids a liquidity crisis, and the federal funds rate remains stable.

In 2008, healthy banks refused to borrow from the discount window even when they needed liquidity because they feared stigma. The Fed created the Term Auction Facility (TAF) to lend anonymously, and later the Primary Dealer Credit Facility (PDCF) for investment banks. These facilities used different names and auction mechanisms to avoid the stigma associated with the discount window.

classifications.value: Regulations requiring banks to hold a minimum fraction of deposits as reserves; affects the money multiplier
characteristics: Required reserves = reserve ratio × deposits ($RR = rr \times D$)
Excess reserves = actual reserves - required reserves
Lowering rr increases excess reserves → more lending → money multiplier increases → money supply expands
Raising rr decreases excess reserves → less lending → money multiplier decreases → money supply contracts
Money multiplier: $m = (1+c)/(rr+c+e)$
Many central banks have eliminated reserve requirements (Canada 1992, UK 1981, Australia 1999, Sweden 1994, Fed 2020)
classifications.behavior:
Reserve requirements were once a key monetary policy tool, but they have fallen out of favor. The Fed actively used reserve requirements in the 1970s and 1980s, raising rr to tighten policy and lowering rr to ease policy. However, reserve requirements have several disadvantages: (1) they act as a tax on banks (banks earn no interest on required reserves), distorting financial markets; (2) they are a blunt instrument (changes affect all banks at once); (3) banks can circumvent them through financial innovation (sweep accounts, off-balance-sheet activities); (4) in an ample reserves framework, reserve requirements are irrelevant because banks already hold large excess reserves.

In March 2020, the Fed reduced reserve requirement ratios to 0% for all deposits, effectively eliminating reserve requirements. This was a long time coming: the trend toward zero reserve requirements began in the 1990s as banks learned to avoid them via sweep accounts (automatically sweeping funds from checking to savings to reduce required reserves). Today, the Fed relies on IORB rather than reserve requirements to control the money supply and interest rates.

Other central banks eliminated reserve requirements even earlier: Canada eliminated them in 1992, the UK in 1981, Australia in 1999, Sweden in 1994. The ECB still has reserve requirements (1% as of 2024) but pays interest on them, reducing the distortion. China actively uses reserve requirements as a policy tool, raising them to cool inflation and lowering them to stimulate growth.

image.alt: Graph: Money multiplier (m) as function of reserve ratio (rr); as rr increases, m decreases (hyperbolic relationship); Fed's 2020 reduction of rr to 0 makes m = (1+c)/(c+e).
image.caption: Figure 8.1.6: Reserve requirements affect the money multiplier; lowering rr increases the multiplier, expanding the money supply.

examples.explanation: In 1992, the Fed lowered reserve requirements from 12% to 10% on transaction deposits. This freed up about \$9 billion in reserves, allowing banks to lend more. The money multiplier increased, and the money supply expanded, helping the recovery from the 1990-1991 recession.

In March 2020, the Fed reduced reserve requirement ratios to 0% for all deposits. This was largely symbolic, as banks already held trillions in excess reserves. The move simplified compliance and eliminated the implicit tax on banks, but had little effect on the money supply or lending.

classifications.type: Modern Tools: IORB, ON RRP, and SRF
classifications.value: Tools developed after 2008 to implement policy in an ample reserves framework
characteristics: IORB (Interest on Reserve Balances): Paid on all reserves (required and excess). Acts as a floor for the federal funds rate.
ON RRP (Overnight Reverse Repo Facility): Allows non-banks (money market funds) to lend reserves to the Fed at a rate slightly below IORB. Provides a floor for non-banks.
SRF (Standing Repo Facility): A permanent backstop for repo markets, allowing primary dealers to borrow reserves against Treasury securities at a fixed rate.
These tools allow the Fed to control interest rates without actively managing reserve supply.
IORB is the primary tool; ON RRP and SRF are supporting tools.
classifications.behavior:
In the ample reserves framework (post-2008), the Fed no longer uses active OMOs to fine-tune reserve supply. Instead, it sets IORB (interest on reserves) as a floor under the federal funds rate. Banks will not lend reserves to other banks at rates below IORB because they can earn IORB risk-free by holding reserves. However, non-banks (money market funds, GSEs) cannot earn IORB. For them, the Fed offers the ON RRP facility, paying a rate slightly below IORB. This creates a two-tier floor: IORB for banks, ON RRP for non-banks. The federal funds rate (which only involves banks) typically sits between the ON RRP rate and IORB.

The SRF (Standing Repo Facility) was created after the 2019 repo spike to prevent a repeat. The SRF allows primary dealers to borrow reserves overnight against Treasury securities at a fixed rate (initially 25bp above IORB). This provides a ceiling for repo rates and ensures that liquidity shocks do not cause extreme rate spikes.

These modern tools have made traditional OMOs largely obsolete for daily operations. The Fed's balance sheet is now huge (\$7-8 trillion), and reserves are abundant. The Fed conducts OMOs only for occasional adjustments (e.g., to manage year-end funding pressures) or as part of QE/QT.

image.alt: Graph: IORB floor (5.40%), ON RRP floor (5.30%), federal funds rate trading between them; money supply horizontal at ample reserves level.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.7: The ample reserves floor system: IORB sets the floor for banks, ON RRP sets the floor for non-banks; the federal funds rate trades between them.

examples.explanation: To tighten policy, the Fed raises IORB from 5.40% to 5.65%. Banks earn more on reserves, so they are less willing to lend reserves at low rates. The federal funds rate rises to 5.65%. To ease policy, the Fed lowers IORB. The Fed uses IORB as its primary tool to implement the FOMC's target range.

Money market funds have excess cash. They can lend to banks at 5.30% (ON RRP rate) or buy Treasury bills at 5.25%. They choose the ON RRP facility because it is risk-free. This drains cash from money funds, keeping the federal funds rate from falling below 5.30%.

During a liquidity shock, repo rates spike to 6% while the SRF rate is 5.50%. Primary dealers can borrow from the SRF at 5.50% against Treasury collateral, capping rates. The SRF is always available, so the 2019 repo spike cannot recur.

principles.principle: Open Market Operations Are the Primary Tool for Implementing Monetary Policy, But Their Role Has Changed with the Ample Reserves Framework
principles.statement: Before 2008, the Fed used daily open market operations to fine-tune reserve supply, keeping the federal funds rate at its target. This 'scarce reserves' framework required precise forecasting of reserve demand. After 2008, with abundant reserves (due to QE), the Fed shifted to an 'ample reserves' framework where IORB (interest on reserves) is the primary tool, and OMOs are used only occasionally. In the new framework, the Fed sets IORB and lets reserves adjust endogenously. The money supply is no longer the operating target; the interest rate is. This change means that the size of the Fed's balance sheet is no longer a direct indicator of policy stance. The Fed can have \$3 trillion in reserves and tight policy (high IORB) or easy policy (low IORB).
principles.application: Monetary policy analysts should not interpret an increase in reserves (due to QE) as expansionary policy in the ample reserves framework. Instead, focus on IORB and the Fed's forward guidance. The Fed's balance sheet is a tool for managing long-term rates (via QE) and providing liquidity, not for signaling the policy stance. When analyzing OMOs, distinguish between temporary operations (repos, which manage daily liquidity) and permanent operations (outright purchases, which are part of QE or QT).
principles.example: From 2008-2014, the Fed expanded its balance sheet from \$900 billion to \$4.5 trillion via QE (permanent OMOs). Yet the federal funds rate remained near zero (easy policy). From 2017-2019, the Fed engaged in QT (selling bonds), reducing reserves to \$2.5 trillion, while raising the fed funds rate to 2.5% (tight policy). The size of the balance sheet did not indicate the policy stance; IORB and the fed funds target did.

image.alt: Graph: Fed balance sheet size (trillions) and federal funds rate (%). Balance sheet expands 2008-2014 while rate at zero; balance sheet shrinks 2017-2019 while rate rises; no correlation.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.8: In the ample reserves framework, the size of the Fed's balance sheet is not a reliable indicator of policy stance; IORB and the fed funds rate matter more.

principles.principle: The Discount Window is a Critical Backstop for Financial Stability, But Stigma Limits Its Use
principles.pattern: The discount window is the Fed's lender of last resort facility, designed to prevent liquidity crises from becoming solvency crises. However, banks are reluctant to borrow from the discount window because they fear market perception of weakness (stigma). This stigma undermines the facility's effectiveness: banks may run into liquidity problems rather than borrow, leading to fires sales, contagion, and systemic crises. The Fed has tried to reduce stigma by renaming the facility, simplifying procedures, and encouraging use, but stigma persists. During the 2008 crisis and the 2023 regional banking crisis, banks avoided the discount window, forcing the Fed to create alternative facilities (TAF, BTFP) with different names to circumvent stigma. Policymakers continue to debate how to eliminate discount window stigma.
principles.application: Financial stability analysts should monitor discount window usage as an indicator of banking system stress. An increase in borrowing signals liquidity pressures. However, the absence of borrowing does not mean no stress—banks may be avoiding the window due to stigma. The Fed's creation of alternative facilities (with different names) provides a workaround. For bank supervisors, reducing stigma through transparency (disclosing borrowing with lags) and normalizing discount window use as routine (not exceptional) could help.
principles.example: In March 2023, Silicon Valley Bank and Signature Bank failed. Neither borrowed from the discount window in the days before failure, despite facing massive deposit outflows. The FDIC had to step in. The Fed then created the Bank Term Funding Program (BTFP), which allowed banks to borrow at par against bonds (unlike the discount window, which applied haircuts). Banks used the BTFP heavily (over \$100 billion in days) but still avoided the discount window. Stigma remained a powerful force.

image.alt: Graph: Discount window borrowing (2007-2024); spikes during 2008 crisis; near-zero usage 2010-2023; small spike during 2023 regional banking crisis (but BTFP usage much larger).
image.caption: Figure 8.1.9: Discount window borrowing remains low due to stigma; the Fed created alternative facilities (TAF, BTFP) to circumvent the stigma problem.

worked_examples.title: Calculating the Effects of Open Market Operations on Reserves and the Money Supply

image.alt: Table: Initial reserves $100B, rr=10%, c=20%, e=5%; OMO purchase of $20B adds reserves; new deposits = multiplier × ΔMB.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.10: Worked example: calculating the money supply effect of an open market purchase.

worked_examples.problem:
The Fed conducts an open market purchase of \$20 billion in Treasury bonds. Assume the reserve requirement is $rr = 10\%$, the currency-deposit ratio is $c = 20\%$, and the excess reserves ratio is $e = 5\%$.

a) Calculate the realistic money multiplier.
b) Calculate the maximum potential increase in deposits (M1) from this OMO.
c) Calculate the maximum potential increase in loans.
d) If banks initially hold excess reserves of \$50 billion, how does that affect the actual deposit expansion?
e) If the Fed instead conducted an open market sale of \$10 billion, what would be the effect on deposits?

step_by_step.explanation:
Open market purchase of \$20 billion injects \$20 billion in reserves (monetary base increases by \$20 billion).

Maximum deposit increase: $\Delta D = m \times \Delta MB = 3.43 \times \$20B = \$68.6\text{ billion}$
Loans increase by deposits minus reserves held: $\Delta L = \Delta D - (rr+e)\Delta D = \$68.6B - (0.15 \times \$68.6B) = \$68.6B - \$10.29B = \$58.31\text{ billion}$

If banks already have excess reserves of \$50 billion, they do not need the new reserves to lend. The OMO adds reserves to an already abundant system. In an ample reserves framework (post-2008), the OMO has no effect on lending or deposits because banks simply hold the new reserves as excess reserves (earning IORB). The money multiplier becomes irrelevant when $e$ is large. In a scarce reserves framework, the OMO would increase lending as described, but with ample reserves, the effect is near zero.

This is why the Fed no longer uses OMOs to fine-tune policy in the ample reserves era. The marginal effect of a reserve injection is negligible when reserves are already abundant.

step_by_step.step: Effect of open market sale of $10 billion
step_by_step.explanation:
An open market sale of \$10 billion drains reserves, decreasing the monetary base by \$10 billion.

Maximum deposit decrease: $\Delta D = m \times \Delta MB = 3.43 \times (-\$10B) = -\$34.3\text{ billion}$

Maximum loan decrease: $\Delta L = -\$34.3B - (0.15 \times -\$34.3B) = -\$34.3B + \$5.15B = -\$29.15\text{ billion}$

Again, in an ample reserves framework, the effect is much smaller because banks can use excess reserves to absorb the drain without changing lending.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) m = 3.43. b) ΔD = \$68.6B. c) ΔL = \$58.31B. d) With ample excess reserves, OMO has minimal effect on deposits (banks hold extra reserves as excess). e) Open market sale reduces deposits by \$34.3B (in scarce reserves framework).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the money multiplier to quantify the effects of open market operations on deposits and loans, and recognizing the difference between scarce and ample reserves frameworks.

worked_examples.title: Discount Window vs. Alternative Facilities: Stigma and Policy Design

image.alt: Table: Comparison of discount window (primary credit rate = 5.50%, stigma high, collateral haircuts apply) vs. BTFP (rate = 5.00%, stigma lower, no haircuts).
image.caption: Figure 8.1.11: Worked example: comparing discount window terms with alternative liquidity facilities.

worked_examples.problem:
During a liquidity crisis, a regional bank faces a sudden deposit outflow of \$500 million. The bank has Treasury bonds worth \$600 million (market value). The Fed offers two borrowing options:

Discount Window (Primary Credit): Rate = 5.50% (fed funds target 5.00% + 50bp). Collateral haircut = 5% (bank can borrow 95% of collateral value).

Bank Term Funding Program (BTFP): Rate = 5.00% (par value lending). Collateral valued at par (no haircut) for eligible bonds.

a) How much can the bank borrow from the discount window against its Treasury bonds?
b) How much can the bank borrow from the BTFP?
c) If the bank needs \$500 million, which facility should it use? Why might it still choose the discount window?
d) Explain why the Fed created the BTFP in 2023 rather than just lowering the discount window rate or reducing haircuts.
e) How does stigma affect bank behavior in a crisis?

step_by_step.explanation:
Collateral value = \$600 million. Haircut = 5%, so borrowing capacity = $600M \times (1 - 0.05) = 600M \times 0.95 = \$570\text{ million}$

The bank can borrow up to \$570 million at 5.50%.

BTFP values eligible bonds at par (no haircut). Assuming the Treasury bonds are eligible, borrowing capacity = \$600 million (full face value). Rate = 5.00%.

The bank can borrow up to \$600 million at 5.00%.

The bank needs \$500 million. Both facilities can provide that amount. The BTFP offers a lower rate (5.00% vs. 5.50%) and higher borrowing capacity (no haircut). Rational choice: use BTFP.

However, the bank might still choose the discount window if:
• The bank's bonds are not eligible for BTFP (BTFP had specific eligibility criteria; discount window accepts broader collateral)
• The bank wants to avoid stigma associated with BTFP (though BTFP was designed to have less stigma)
• The bank is already set up for discount window borrowing (BTFP required new applications)

In practice, most banks chose BTFP in 2023, borrowing over \$100 billion.

step_by_step.step: Explain why Fed created BTFP instead of modifying discount window
step_by_step.explanation:
The Fed created the BTFP in March 2023 because the discount window was stigmatized. Banks refused to borrow from the discount window even during the Silicon Valley Bank crisis due to fear of signaling weakness. The Fed could have lowered the discount rate or reduced haircuts, but that would not solve the stigma problem—banks would still avoid the window because borrowing would be public (with a lag) and markets would interpret it as a sign of distress.

The BTFP was designed to circumvent stigma: (1) it was a new facility with a different name, (2) it offered more favorable terms (par lending, no haircuts), and (3) the Fed encouraged its use. The BTFP was also available for a longer term (up to 1 year) than discount window primary credit (overnight). By creating a new facility, the Fed could provide liquidity without the baggage of the discount window's reputation.

After the crisis, the Fed closed the BTFP (March 2024) and has since worked to reduce discount window stigma by encouraging banks to test their access and borrow routinely.

Stigma is the fear that borrowing from the discount window will signal financial weakness to markets, counterparties, and depositors. Banks worry that if they borrow from the discount window:
• Stock price will fall (investors perceive distress)
• Counterparties may demand higher collateral or cut credit lines
• Depositors may withdraw funds (run)

This fear is so strong that banks will often pay higher rates in private markets or sell assets at fire-sale prices rather than borrow from the discount window. This undermines the lender-of-last-resort function and can turn liquidity problems into solvency crises.

The Fed has tried to reduce stigma by:
• Renaming the facility (from 'adjustment credit' to 'primary credit')
• Simplifying documentation and reducing oversight
• Publicly encouraging banks to borrow
• Disclosing borrowing with a 2-year lag (so markets don't know immediately)
• Creating alternative facilities (TAF, BTFP) with different names

Stigma remains a challenge. Some economists argue the Fed should make discount window borrowing automatic for healthy banks (e.g., standing facility) or require banks to borrow periodically to normalize the practice.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) \$570 million. b) \$600 million. c) BTFP (lower rate, higher capacity). d) BTFP circumvented discount window stigma with new name and better terms. e) Stigma causes banks to avoid discount window, undermining lender-of-last-resort function; Fed uses alternative facilities to bypass stigma.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying discount window mechanics and understanding stigma to compare liquidity facilities and analyze policy design during crises.

process_flow.process: FOMC sets target range for federal funds rate
process_flow.description: The Federal Open Market Committee meets 8 times per year to assess economic conditions and set the target for the federal funds rate.
process_flow.mechanism: FOMC decides on a target range (e.g., 5.25% to 5.50%). The Fed's trading desk then implements policy using the tools below.

image.alt: Diagram: FOMC meeting → policy decision → target range (e.g., 5.25-5.50%) → implementation tools.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.12: Step 1 - The FOMC sets the target range for the federal funds rate based on economic conditions.

process_flow.process: Fed uses IORB and ON RRP to set floor under rates
process_flow.description: In the ample reserves framework, the Fed sets IORB (interest on reserves) near the top of the target range and ON RRP near the bottom.
process_flow.mechanism: Banks will not lend reserves below IORB; non-banks will not lend below ON RRP. The federal funds rate trades between these floors.

image.alt: Diagram: IORB set at 5.40%, ON RRP at 5.30%, fed funds target 5.25-5.50%; rates trade between floors.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.13: Step 2 - The Fed sets IORB and ON RRP to create a floor system for interest rates.

process_flow.description: The discount window (primary credit) is set above the target range, acting as a ceiling for overnight rates.
process_flow.mechanism: Banks will not pay more than the discount rate for reserves. If the federal funds rate approaches the discount rate, banks borrow from the discount window, capping the rate.

image.alt: Diagram: Discount rate set at 5.75% (target + 50bp); acts as ceiling; federal funds rate below this.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.14: Step 3 - The discount window acts as a ceiling, preventing overnight rates from rising too high.

process_flow.description: When liquidity shocks occur (e.g., 2019 repo spike), the Fed uses repos or the Standing Repo Facility (SRF) to provide reserves.
process_flow.mechanism: The SRF allows primary dealers to borrow reserves at a fixed rate (e.g., 25bp above IORB), capping repo rates. Discretionary repos are used for larger adjustments.

image.alt: Diagram: SRF rate (5.65%) caps repo rates; during stress, dealers borrow from SRF, preventing spikes.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.15: Step 4 - Repos and the SRF provide a backstop for liquidity shocks, preventing rate spikes.

process_flow.process: Reserve requirements (if any) affect money multiplier
process_flow.description: In jurisdictions with positive reserve requirements, changes in rr affect the money multiplier and lending capacity.
process_flow.mechanism: Lower rr increases excess reserves, raising the money multiplier; higher rr decreases excess reserves, lowering the multiplier. The Fed set rr to zero in 2020, so this tool is no longer active in the US.

image.alt: Diagram: rr set to 0% (Fed); no effect on money multiplier; other central banks (e.g., China) still use rr actively.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.16: Step 5 - Reserve requirements are now zero in the US but remain active in some other economies.

image.alt: Master framework: FOMC sets target → IORB/ON RRP (floor) + discount window (ceiling) + SRF (repo backstop) + OMOs (occasional) + reserve requirements (zero) → federal funds rate within target range → transmission to economy.
image.caption: Figure 8.1.17: Complete framework for monetary policy tools: from FOMC decision to interest rate implementation.

summary: Central banks use a toolkit of instruments to implement monetary policy. The three traditional tools are open market operations (purchases and sales of government securities to adjust reserves and interest rates), the discount rate (the interest rate at which banks borrow reserves directly from the central bank, acting as a liquidity backstop), and reserve requirements (regulations on the fraction of deposits banks must hold as reserves, affecting the money multiplier). Since the 2008 financial crisis, the Fed has shifted from a 'scarce reserves' framework (active OMOs) to an 'ample reserves' floor system where Interest on Reserve Balances (IORB) is the primary tool, supported by the Overnight Reverse Repo Facility (ON RRP) for non-banks and the Standing Repo Facility (SRF) as a backstop. The discount window remains a lender-of-last-resort facility but is underused due to stigma. Reserve requirements were set to zero by the Fed in 2020 and have been eliminated by many other central banks. Understanding these tools is essential for analyzing how central banks control interest rates, provide liquidity during crises, and influence the money supply and credit conditions.
